  • Changelog

Liposomal amphotericin B (Hong 2006)

Source: vignettes/articles/Hong_2006_amphotericinB_liposomal.Rmd
Hong_2006_amphotericinB_liposomal.Rmd

Model and source

  • Citation: Hong Y, Shaw PJ, Nath CE, Yadav SP, Stephen KR, Earl JW, McLachlan AJ. Population pharmacokinetics of liposomal amphotericin B in pediatric patients with malignant diseases. Antimicrob Agents Chemother. 2006 Mar;50(3):935-942. doi:10.1128/AAC.50.3.935-942.2006
  • Description: Two-compartment population PK model for liposomal amphotericin B (AmBisome) in 39 pediatric oncology patients receiving 1-h IV infusions (Hong 2006). Clearance and central volume scale exponentially with body weight centered at the cohort-median 21 kg; the paper additionally reports substantial between-occasion variability on CL and V1 that is encoded here as IIV on fixed-at-1 multiplicative anchors (Bellanti 2015 IOV-as-IIV pattern).
  • Article: Antimicrob Agents Chemother 2006;50(3):935-942 (open access)

Population

The model was developed from a prospective, single-centre, open-label observational pharmacokinetic study of 39 paediatric oncology patients at the Children’s Hospital at Westmead (Sydney, NSW, Australia) (Hong 2006 Table 1). Patients were all neutropenic and febrile, with a mix of diagnoses (15 acute lymphoblastic leukaemia, 4 acute myeloid leukaemia, 20 other), and 16 of the 39 had received a bone marrow transplant (12 allogeneic, 4 autologous). Liposomal amphotericin B (AmBisome) was administered once daily as a 1-h intravenous infusion through a central venous catheter at doses ranging from 0.8 to 5.9 mg/kg/day. A total of 637 plasma concentration observations were collected over 48 dosing courses (mean 13.3 samples per course, range 3-30). Baseline demographics (Hong 2006 Table 1, mean (SD), median, range): age 7.1 years (5.1), 6.5, 0.17-17; weight 28.8 kg (19.8), 21.1, 6.1-84.1; height 119.2 cm (33.7), 118.5, 61.5-190.0; body surface area 0.96 m^2 (0.46), 0.82, 0.32-2.11; serum creatinine 74 umol/L (108), 47, 19-659; gamma glutamyl transferase 105 U/L (211), 39, 11-1398. The cohort was 67% male. The model was fit in NONMEM 5.1.1 with first-order conditional estimation, with lognormal IIV on CL, V1, Q, and V2, additional between-occasion variability (BOV) on CL and V1, and a combined additive plus proportional residual-error model. Robustness and accuracy were verified by a bootstrap of 1000 successful replicates in Wings for NONMEM.

The same information is available programmatically via readModelDb("Hong_2006_amphotericinB_liposomal")$population.

Source trace

Every numeric value in ini() carries an in-file comment pointing to the Hong 2006 source location. The table below collects them in one place for review.

Equation / parameter Value Source location
lcl (CL) 0.44 L/h Table 3 row “CL (l h-1)” (RSE 27%); Table 4 row “theta CL”
lvc (V1) 3.12 L Table 3 row “V1 (l)” (RSE 40%); Table 4 row “theta V1”
lq (Q) 0.73 L/h Table 3 row “Q (l h-1)” (RSE 18%); Table 4 row “theta Q”
lvp (V2) 18.0 L Table 3 row “V2 (l)” (RSE 40%); Table 4 row “theta V2”
e_wt_cl (theta_5) 0.0152 /kg Table 3 row “theta_5”; Table 4 row “theta CL ~ WT” (RSE 12%)
e_wt_vc (theta_6) 0.0241 /kg Table 3 row “theta_6”; Table 4 row “theta V1 ~ WT” (RSE 29%)
etalcl (omega^2 CL = 0.0108) 0.0108 Table 3 CL IIV 10%; Table 4 omega^2 CL
etalvc (omega^2 V1 = 0.0450) 0.0450 Table 4 bootstrap omega^2 V1 (original failed; see notes below)
etalq (omega^2 Q = 0.593) 0.593 Table 3 Q IIV 77%; Table 4 omega^2 Q
etalvp (omega^2 V2 = 0.546) 0.546 Table 3 V2 IIV 74%; Table 4 omega^2 V2
etalcl_bov_mult (pi^2 = 0.209) 0.209 Table 3 CL BOV 46%; Table 4 pi^2 CL
etalvc_bov_mult (pi^2 = 0.319) 0.319 Table 3 V1 BOV 56%; Table 4 pi^2 V1
propSd (27% proportional) 0.27 Table 3 row “Proportional (%)” (RSE 7%); Table 4 sigma^2 prop
addSd (0.034 mg/L additive) 0.034 Table 3 row “Additive (mg l-1)” (RSE 24%); Table 4 sigma^2 add
WT centering (21 kg) 21 Methods text after Table 2 footnote: WTmedian = 21 kg
2-cmt IV zero-order infusion n/a Abstract; Results “Population pharmacokinetic modeling” para 1
Combined add + prop residual n/a Methods Eq. (combined error model); Results para 2
Lognormal IIV on CL/V1/Q/V2 n/a Methods “Development of base population pharmacokinetic model”
BOV on CL and V1 (BLOCK(2) SAME) n/a Methods Eq. (BOV definition); Results para 2

IIV / BOV variance derivation. Hong 2006 reports IIV in Table 3 as a percentage; the corresponding numbers in Table 4 are omega^2 (the variance on the log scale). The “%” values in Table 3 therefore represent the log-scale SD (omega) expressed as a percent, so omega^2 ~ ("%"/100)^2:

  • CL: 10% IIV -> omega^2 = 0.0108 (Table 4)
  • V1: failed-to-estimate in Table 3; bootstrap omega^2 = 0.0450 (Table 4)
  • Q: 77% IIV -> omega^2 = 0.593 (Table 4)
  • V2: 74% IIV -> omega^2 = 0.546 (Table 4)
  • CL BOV: 46% -> pi^2 = 0.209 (Table 4)
  • V1 BOV: 56% -> pi^2 = 0.319 (Table 4)

Virtual cohort

Original observed data are not publicly available. The cohort below covers four scenarios bracketing the paper’s body-weight range: a typical patient at the cohort median weight (WT = 21 kg), a small infant (WT = 10 kg), a school-aged child (WT = 35 kg), and an adolescent (WT = 60 kg). All scenarios receive a 1-h IV infusion of liposomal amphotericin B once daily for 7 doses at 3 mg/kg/day (the mid-range prophylactic regimen described in Hong 2006 Methods and Results).

set.seed(20260531)

n_sub <- 200L

build_arm <- function(label, wt_kg, dose_mg_per_kg_per_day, id_offset) {
  ids <- id_offset + seq_len(n_sub)

  dose_amt_mg <- wt_kg * dose_mg_per_kg_per_day
  infusion_h  <- 1
  dose_times  <- seq(0, 144, by = 24)        # seven doses, once daily

  dose_rows <- tidyr::expand_grid(id = ids, time = dose_times) |>
    mutate(
      evid     = 1L,
      amt      = dose_amt_mg,
      cmt      = "central",
      rate     = dose_amt_mg / infusion_h,    # 1-h IV infusion
      cohort   = label,
      WT       = wt_kg,
      dose_mgkg = dose_mg_per_kg_per_day
    )

  obs_times <- c(seq(0, 24, by = 0.25),
                 seq(25, 168, by = 2),
                 seq(170, 240, by = 4))
  obs_rows <- tidyr::expand_grid(id = ids, time = obs_times) |>
    mutate(
      evid     = 0L,
      amt      = 0,
      cmt      = NA_character_,
      rate     = 0,
      cohort   = label,
      WT       = wt_kg,
      dose_mgkg = dose_mg_per_kg_per_day
    )

  bind_rows(dose_rows, obs_rows) |> arrange(id, time, desc(evid))
}

events <- bind_rows(
  build_arm("infant_10kg",      10, 3,    0L),
  build_arm("typical_21kg",     21, 3,  200L),
  build_arm("schoolage_35kg",   35, 3,  400L),
  build_arm("adolescent_60kg",  60, 3,  600L)
)

stopifnot(!anyDuplicated(unique(events[, c("id", "time", "evid")])))

Simulation 

mod <- readModelDb("Hong_2006_amphotericinB_liposomal")

sim <- rxode2::rxSolve(
  mod,
  events = events,
  keep   = c("cohort", "WT", "dose_mgkg")
) |> as.data.frame()
#> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()'

For typical-value comparisons against the Hong 2006 Table 3 point estimates, also simulate with the random effects zeroed:

mod_typical <- mod |> rxode2::zeroRe()
#> ℹ parameter labels from comments will be replaced by 'label()'

sim_typical <- rxode2::rxSolve(
  mod_typical,
  events = events,
  keep   = c("cohort", "WT", "dose_mgkg")
) |> as.data.frame()
#> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalq', 'etalvp', 'etalcl_bov_mult', 'etalvc_bov_mult'
#> Warning: multi-subject simulation without without 'omega'

Concentration-time profile (typical-value, 1.8 mg/kg)

Hong 2006 Figure 2 shows the representative concentration-time profile of a patient treated with 1-h IV infusions of L-AmB at 1.8 mg/kg per day over a single 24-h dosing interval. To reproduce that figure, we simulate a typical 21-kg patient (cohort median) at the 1.8 mg/kg regimen, then plot the first 24 hours after dose 1.

events_fig2 <- build_arm("fig2_1.8mgkg", 21, 1.8, 0L)
sim_fig2    <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod_typical, events = events_fig2,
                               keep = c("cohort", "WT", "dose_mgkg")) |>
  as.data.frame()
#> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalq', 'etalvp', 'etalcl_bov_mult', 'etalvc_bov_mult'
#> Warning: multi-subject simulation without without 'omega'

sim_fig2 |>
  filter(time <= 24, id == min(id)) |>
  ggplot(aes(time, Cc)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  labs(
    x = "Time after first dose (h)",
    y = "L-AmB plasma concentration (mg/L)",
    title = "Replicates Hong 2006 Figure 2: typical-value Cc over one 24-h interval",
    subtitle = "1-h IV infusion of L-AmB at 1.8 mg/kg in a typical 21 kg patient"
  )

Concentration-time profile by weight cohort 

sim |>
  group_by(cohort, time) |>
  summarise(
    Q05 = quantile(Cc, 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),
    Q50 = quantile(Cc, 0.50, na.rm = TRUE),
    Q95 = quantile(Cc, 0.95, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(time, Q50, colour = cohort, fill = cohort)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Q05, ymax = Q95), alpha = 0.2, colour = NA) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(
    x = "Time (h)",
    y = "Simulated L-AmB Cc (mg/L, log scale)",
    title = "Simulated L-AmB by weight cohort, 3 mg/kg/day x 7 doses",
    caption = "Lines: median; ribbons: 5th-95th percentile across 200 simulated subjects."
  )
#> Warning in scale_y_log10(): log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.
#> log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.
#> log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.
#> log-10 transformation introduced infinite values.

PKNCA validation (steady-state, last dosing interval)

The block below characterises steady-state Cmax, Cmin (= Ctau), AUC0-tau, average concentration, and the inferred terminal half-life from the typical-value time course over the seventh dosing interval (tau = 24 h). The treatment grouping is cohort, matching the four weight-stratified scenarios.

last_dose_time <- 144  # seventh dose at t = 144 h; tau = 24 h

sim_nca <- sim_typical |>
  filter(!is.na(Cc), time >= last_dose_time, time <= last_dose_time + 24) |>
  mutate(time_in_tau = time - last_dose_time) |>
  select(id, time = time_in_tau, Cc, cohort)

dose_df <- events |>
  filter(evid == 1, time == last_dose_time) |>
  select(id, time, amt, cohort) |>
  mutate(time = 0)

conc_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAconc(sim_nca, Cc ~ time | cohort + id,
                             concu = "mg/L", timeu = "hr")
dose_obj <- PKNCA::PKNCAdose(dose_df, amt ~ time | cohort + id,
                             doseu = "mg")

intervals <- data.frame(
  start    = 0,
  end      = 24,
  cmax     = TRUE,
  tmax     = TRUE,
  cmin     = TRUE,
  auclast  = TRUE,
  cav      = TRUE
)

nca_res <- PKNCA::pk.nca(
  PKNCA::PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = intervals)
)
#> Warning: Requesting an AUC range starting (0) before the first measurement (1) is not allowed

nca_summary <- summary(nca_res)
knitr::kable(
  nca_summary,
  caption = "Simulated steady-state NCA parameters (typical-value, seventh dose interval, tau = 24 h) by weight cohort."
)
Simulated steady-state NCA parameters (typical-value, seventh dose interval, tau = 24 h) by weight cohort.
Interval Start Interval End cohort N AUClast (hr*mg/L) Cmax (mg/L) Cmin (mg/L) Tmax (hr) Cav (mg/L)
0 24 adolescent_60kg 200 NC 24.7 [0.000] 4.45 [0.000] 1.00 [1.00, 1.00] NC
0 24 infant_10kg 200 NC 11.7 [0.000] 1.81 [0.000] 1.00 [1.00, 1.00] NC
0 24 schoolage_35kg 200 NC 24.6 [0.000] 4.04 [0.000] 1.00 [1.00, 1.00] NC
0 24 typical_21kg 200 NC 19.8 [0.000] 3.13 [0.000] 1.00 [1.00, 1.00] NC

Terminal half-life (typical-value)

Hong 2006 reports a secondary terminal half-life t1/2,beta = 59.4 SD 36.5 h derived from individual Bayesian posterior estimates. For a typical 2-compartment IV model with CL = 0.44 L/h, V1 = 3.12 L, Q = 0.73 L/h, V2 = 18.0 L, the two macro-rate constants alpha and beta satisfy alpha + beta = kel + k12 + k21 and alpha * beta = kel * k21, giving beta ~= 0.0142 1/h and t1/2,beta ~= 48.8 h for the typical (21 kg) subject. The figure below evaluates the post-infusion log-linear decline numerically from the stopped-dose simulation.

event_singledose <- build_arm("typical_21kg_single", 21, 3, 0L) |>
  filter(time <= 200, !(evid == 1 & time > 0))      # one dose only
sim_single <- rxode2::rxSolve(mod_typical, events = event_singledose,
                              keep = c("WT")) |>
  as.data.frame()
#> ℹ omega/sigma items treated as zero: 'etalcl', 'etalvc', 'etalq', 'etalvp', 'etalcl_bov_mult', 'etalvc_bov_mult'
#> Warning: multi-subject simulation without without 'omega'

# Estimate terminal slope on natural log scale from the 72-200 h window
tail_fit <- sim_single |>
  filter(id == min(id), time >= 72, time <= 200, Cc > 0) |>
  mutate(lnCc = log(Cc))
fit <- lm(lnCc ~ time, data = tail_fit)
lambda_z <- -coef(fit)[["time"]]
tbeta_h <- log(2) / lambda_z

knitr::kable(
  tibble(
    quantity = c(
      "Simulated terminal slope lambda_z (1/h)",
      "Simulated terminal half-life t1/2,beta (h)",
      "Published t1/2,beta (mean SD 1/h)",
      "Analytical beta from typical THETAs (1/h)",
      "Analytical t1/2,beta from typical THETAs (h)"
    ),
    value = c(
      sprintf("%.4f", lambda_z),
      sprintf("%.1f", tbeta_h),
      "59.4 +/- 36.5",
      "0.0142",
      "48.8"
    )
  ),
  caption = "Simulated vs published terminal half-life for the typical 21 kg subject."
)
Simulated vs published terminal half-life for the typical 21 kg subject.
quantity value
Simulated terminal slope lambda_z (1/h) 0.0143
Simulated terminal half-life t1/2,beta (h) 48.6
Published t1/2,beta (mean SD 1/h) 59.4 +/- 36.5
Analytical beta from typical THETAs (1/h) 0.0142
Analytical t1/2,beta from typical THETAs (h) 48.8

Steady-state volume of distribution (typical-value)

Hong 2006 reports a secondary Vss = 1.14 +/- 0.68 L/kg from individual Bayesian estimates. For the typical 21 kg subject the model gives Vss = (V1 + V2) / WT = (3.12 + 18.0) / 21 = 1.006 L/kg, comparable to the published mean.

vc_typ <- 3.12
vp_typ <- 18.0
wt_typ <- 21
vss_lkg <- (vc_typ + vp_typ) / wt_typ
knitr::kable(
  tibble(
    quantity = c(
      "Simulated Vss for typical 21 kg subject (L/kg)",
      "Published Vss (mean SD 1/kg)"
    ),
    value = c(
      sprintf("%.3f", vss_lkg),
      "1.14 +/- 0.68"
    )
  ),
  caption = "Simulated vs published Vss for the typical 21 kg subject."
)
Simulated vs published Vss for the typical 21 kg subject.
quantity value
Simulated Vss for typical 21 kg subject (L/kg) 1.006
Published Vss (mean SD 1/kg) 1.14 +/- 0.68

Cmax,ss / MIC target attainment

Hong 2006 demonstrated that Cmax,ss / MIC discriminates between complete and partial clinical responses to L-AmB in patients with proven invasive fungal infection (Hong 2006 Table 5 and Figure 3). The authors then used the population PK model to explore whether weight-stratified dosing achieves the proposed target of Cmax,ss / MIC > 50 for canonical MICs of 0.5 mg/L (Candida) and 1.0 mg/L (Aspergillus). The table below reproduces that calculation using the typical-value Cmax,ss at the seventh dose interval for the four weight cohorts at 3 mg/kg/day.

cmax_ss <- sim_typical |>
  filter(time >= last_dose_time, time <= last_dose_time + 24) |>
  group_by(cohort, WT, dose_mgkg) |>
  summarise(Cmax_ss_mgL = max(Cc, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(
    Cmaxss_over_MIC_Candida    = round(Cmax_ss_mgL / 0.5, 1),
    Cmaxss_over_MIC_Aspergillus = round(Cmax_ss_mgL / 1.0, 1)
  )

knitr::kable(
  cmax_ss,
  digits = 2,
  caption = "Typical-value Cmax,ss and Cmax,ss/MIC ratios by weight cohort at 3 mg/kg/day, day 7. Hong 2006 proposed Cmax,ss/MIC >= 50 as the antifungal efficacy target."
)
Typical-value Cmax,ss and Cmax,ss/MIC ratios by weight cohort at 3 mg/kg/day, day 7. Hong 2006 proposed Cmax,ss/MIC >= 50 as the antifungal efficacy target.
cohort WT dose_mgkg Cmax_ss_mgL Cmaxss_over_MIC_Candida Cmaxss_over_MIC_Aspergillus
adolescent_60kg 60 3 24.66 49.3 24.7
infant_10kg 10 3 11.75 23.5 11.7
schoolage_35kg 35 3 24.62 49.2 24.6
typical_21kg 21 3 19.78 39.6 19.8

Assumptions and deviations

  • Between-occasion variability (BOV) encoded as IIV on multiplicative anchors. Hong 2006 estimates BOV on CL (46%) and V1 (56%) via NONMEM’s $OMEGA BLOCK(2) SAME (a single magnitude shared across all dosing occasions for a given subject). nlmixr2lib has no canonical pattern for occasion-keyed IOV without an OCC covariate column, so the BOV variance is carried here as ordinary IIV on fixed-at-1 multiplicative anchors lcl_bov_mult and lvc_bov_mult (following the Bellanti 2015 deferoxamine precedent). The total log-scale variance for the typical subject is therefore etalcl + etalcl_bov_mult = 0.0108 + 0.209 = 0.220 (CL) and `etalvc + etalvc_bov_mult = 0.045

    • 0.319 = 0.364` (V1). The model preserves the population-level spread but loses the within-subject between-occasion drift that the paper estimates (a single simulated subject does not switch parameter values between courses).

  • IIV on V1 from the bootstrap. Hong 2006 Table 3 leaves the IIV V1 column blank because the original NONMEM fit did not estimate a stable omega^2 for V1 (the Discussion notes that BOV exceeded IIV on V1, and NONMEM had difficulty disentangling them). The bootstrap mean from 1000 successful replicates (omega^2 V1 = 0.0450, Table

    1. is used here as the best available point estimate. Treat this number as a lower bound on the V1 IIV; the paper does not commit to a published value.

  • Weight is time-varying across dosing courses. Hong 2006 Methods states that covariate values were updated per course; the model encodes WT as time-varying. For the simulations above we hold WT fixed within each cohort (matched to the median weight for that band). Users simulating real per-patient time-varying WT should supply the column at every record.

  • No erratum identified. A Crossref search for “Hong 2006 liposomal amphotericin erratum / corrigendum” returned no relevant results in 2026-05-31; the model uses the values as published in the original paper without correction.

  • Race / ethnicity not reported. Hong 2006 does not report race / ethnicity for the cohort (the study was conducted at a single centre in Sydney). No race-based stratification or covariate is included.

  • Renal-function markers not retained. The paper screened serum creatinine and other renal markers as covariates but did not retain them because renal elimination is a minor pathway for L-AmB (Hong 2006 Discussion). The model has no renal-function covariate.

  • Sex not retained. Sex did not significantly affect L-AmB PK in the Mann-Whitney U test (Hong 2006 Results). The model has no sex covariate.

On this page

Developed by Richard Hooijmaijers, Matthew Fidler, Bill Denney, Kiranmayi Vedantham.
